title: Choosing a study design: assignment, selection, and time

objectives.statement: Classify experiments, prospective and retrospective cohorts, case-control studies, cross-sectional studies, and natural experiments from assignment, selection, and timing features.
Choose and justify a best-fit study design for a new question under stated ethical, temporal, outcome-frequency, and data-availability constraints.
State the strongest warranted inference and a material boundary for experimental, ordinary observational, and natural-experiment comparisons.

scenes.title: Who creates the comparison?
scenes.contentHtml:
Who creates the comparison?

Two campus residence halls report different morning-alertness scores. Hall A introduced quiet hours last month; Hall B did not. A dean asks, “Did quiet hours improve alertness?”

You cannot answer honestly from the score difference alone. Students may have chosen Hall A because they already preferred quiet evenings. The halls may differ in class schedules or distance from late-night venues. If researchers instead assigned eligible volunteers by chance to the two policies and measured them in the same way, the same score difference could support a stronger claim.

The design changes what the comparison means.

In this lesson, you will learn to choose among five ways of making a comparison: an experiment, a cohort study, a case-control study, a cross-sectional study, and a natural experiment. The useful question is not “Which label sounds most scientific?” It is “Who created the exposure difference? Did researchers start with exposure, start with outcome, or take one snapshot? What conclusion can that comparison support?”

Retrieve the inquiry chain

Before learning the five choices, rebuild a navigation aid used in the previous lessons. It organizes decisions in this course; it is not a universal sequence that every investigation must follow.

Check
id check-cumulative-chain

Complete the chain before reading on:

observation → bounded empirical question → ___ → conditions → ___ → variable-and-control audit

Then say what each missing step contributes.

A useful completion is hypothesis → prediction. In this course map, the question slot names what needs answering, the hypothesis slot records a proposed explanation or relationship, the conditions qualify that proposal, the prediction slot names the record expected under those conditions, and the variable-and-control audit makes the intended comparison and rival factors visible.

Study design is the next decision: it determines which comparison can actually be made.

First decision: who creates the exposure difference?

The possible cause or condition of interest is the exposure. In the residence case, exposure means living under the quiet-hours policy. The record to be compared is the outcome. Here, the outcome is a morning-alertness score measured in a stated way.

If the research team deliberately assigns the exposure, the study is an experiment. The team might recruit eligible volunteers and assign each to a quiet-hours or usual-hours residence. Random assignment gives each eligible participant a known chance of entering either condition. It makes the groups comparable in expectation, so a well-implemented randomized experiment can support a stronger causal inference than a comparison of self-selected halls. Chance imbalances, departures from the assigned policy, missing records, or unequal measurement can still weaken the inference.

If the research team does not assign exposure, the study is observational. Researchers might follow people who already chose different halls, examine earlier records, or survey students where they live now. An observed association alone does not establish that quiet hours caused the outcome.

A natural experiment uses an exposure difference created by an external event, policy, rule, or implementation process rather than by the research team. Suppose the housing office introduced quiet hours according to a schedule fixed before any alertness scores were known. That creates a possible natural experiment, but the label is not enough. Researchers must explain who received the policy and why. If the schedule also tracks building location or resident preferences, those differences remain rival explanations.

Second decision: where do you enter the timeline?

When exposure was not assigned for the study, imagine a line from exposure to later outcome. The starting point separates three common observational designs.

  • A cohort study begins with a defined population or exposure groups and compares subsequent outcomes. A prospective cohort collects exposure information and follows forward. A retrospective cohort assembles existing records in which exposure was documented before the later outcome.
  • A case-control study begins by selecting cases, which have the outcome, and comparison people called controls, which do not. These controls are not the positive- or negative-control conditions used to check an experiment. Investigators reconstruct earlier exposure in the same way for both groups, and the controls should represent the population that produced the cases. Starting with the outcome can be practical when that outcome is uncommon.
  • A cross-sectional study measures exposure and outcome in a defined population at one time or over one brief survey window. It can estimate prevalence—the proportion with a condition or characteristic at that time—but usually cannot show which variable came first.
Design Who creates or selects the comparison? Central time logic A best-fit question
Experiment Researcher assigns an intervention or exposure assignment → later outcome What is the effect of an ethically assignable intervention?
Cohort Researcher defines a population or exposure groups exposure → subsequent outcome What outcomes develop after a recorded exposure?
Case-control Researcher selects cases and controls by outcome outcome → earlier exposure Which prior exposures differ for an uncommon outcome?
Cross-sectional Researcher samples a population at one time exposure and outcome measured together How common is a characteristic now, and what is it associated with?
Natural experiment An outside event or rule creates exposure groups external assignment → outcome What changed across a defensible outside assignment process?

Read the table row by row and you have the whole comparison; the layout adds nothing extra.

Source Note
claims claim-design-assignment, claim-cohort-direction, claim-case-control-use, claim-cross-sectional-use, claim-randomized-inference, claim-natural-assignment, claim-causal-boundarysources source-study-design-overview, source-clinical-design-essentials, source-observational-designs, source-natural-experiments

The sources support the assignment, selection, timing, and inference distinctions in this decision sequence. Definitions of natural experiments vary; this lesson requires an explicit outside assignment process and cautious causal language. The residence case, decision table, and wording are independently created.

scenes.title: Choose from the comparison you can make
scenes.contentHtml:
Choose from the comparison you can make

The following employer archive is fictional. It contains no participant or patient data, and you do not need to collect personal information. The task is to choose a study design, not to decide whether a real working pattern affects health.

Worked Example
id worked-example-retrospective-cohortlabel Old records with exposure-to-outcome logic

1. State the question.

A fictional employer has complete work-schedule records from 2018 and standardized daytime-alertness assessments from 2022 for the same eligible employees. Did employees recorded on regular night shifts in 2018 have a different chance of low alertness in 2022 than employees recorded on day shifts?

2. Represent the givens and constraints.

Decision feature What the archive provides
Exposure night-shift or day-shift schedule recorded in 2018
Outcome low-alertness status recorded in 2022
Entry into the data all employees who met stated eligibility rules, not people chosen because of alertness status
Assignment the research team did not assign shifts
Practical boundary assigning years of work schedules for this question would not be a responsible or practical classroom proposal

The unknown is the design that matches those features and the strongest conclusion it could support.

3. Choose a plan.

Ask in order: Did researchers assign exposure? If not, did a defensible external process assign it? If neither, where does the archive enter the exposure–outcome timeline?

4. Execute the decision.

Researchers did not assign shifts, so this is not a planned experiment. No external assignment rule has been described, so “the schedules already existed” does not make it a natural experiment.

The archive begins with a defined eligible population, separates employees by a 2018 exposure, and then compares a 2022 outcome. That exposure-to-later-outcome order fits a cohort study.

The cohort is retrospective because the research team assembles it after the schedules and alertness outcomes have already been recorded. Retrospective describes when the study is assembled relative to the events. It does not reverse the cohort's central time logic.

5. Check the nearest alternatives.

  • A prospective cohort could record current shift patterns and follow new alertness outcomes into the future. It fits the same exposure-to-outcome logic but not the already-complete archive.
  • A case-control study would first select employees with low alertness as cases and employees without it as controls, then reconstruct earlier shift exposure. That could reduce how many records need detailed review if low alertness were uncommon, but it is not how this archive enters the population.
  • A cross-sectional study would measure shift status and alertness in the same survey window. It would lose the four-year temporal order already available.
  • A natural experiment would require a specific outside rule that determined shift exposure in a plausibly defensible way. Existing schedules alone do not supply that rule.

6. Interpret the bounded result.

Suppose low alertness is more frequent in the recorded night-shift group. The archive supports an association between the 2018 shift category and the 2022 outcome in this eligible cohort. It does not by itself establish that night shifts caused the difference. Job type, employee choice, schedule changes, or other features might differ between groups, and the records may be incomplete measures of the relevant exposure or outcome.

7. Make the decisive choice explicit.

Why is “the data are old” insufficient for calling this a case-control study?

A defensible answer is that case-control and cohort labels follow how people enter the comparison, not the age of the file. This archive begins with an eligible population and an earlier exposure, then reads toward a later outcome. A case-control study would begin by selecting on the outcome.

Selection and time are separate questions

Use two lines when a label is unclear:

  1. Selection line: Who was eligible, and were people included because of exposure, outcome, a population rule, or a one-time sample?
  2. Time line: Was exposure recorded before the outcome, reconstructed after selecting the outcome, or measured alongside it?

If your two lines disagree with the proposed label, trust the design description rather than a word such as old, follow-up, or natural in the title.

Source Note
claims claim-design-assignment, claim-cohort-direction, claim-case-control-use, claim-cross-sectional-use, claim-natural-assignment, claim-causal-boundarysources source-study-design-overview, source-clinical-design-essentials, source-observational-designs, source-natural-experiments

The sources support exposure-to-outcome cohort logic, outcome-first case-control logic, prospective and retrospective timing, externally created exposure in natural experiments, and cautious interpretation without defensible assignment. The employer archive, decision table, constraints, and result are original fictional teaching material.

scenes.title: Enter the timeline in the right place
scenes.contentHtml:
Enter the timeline in the right place

Begin with retrieval. Without looking back, write two questions:

  1. Who created the exposure difference?
  2. Where did the study enter the exposure–outcome timeline?

Then reconstruct the five design names under the answers. Commit the map before reading the practice feedback.

One part supplied

A fictional materials team can ethically set two curing methods, assign otherwise eligible strips to those methods by chance, hold temperature and strip dimensions alike, and record breaking force.

Which design family fits, and why?

This is a randomized experiment because the research team assigns the curing method by chance and then records the outcome. Random assignment strengthens the comparison by making other starting differences comparable in expectation. The team must still implement both methods as planned and measure breaking force consistently.

Complete the selection lines

Fill the missing entry point before opening the feedback.

Design Entry point Time logic or next step
Prospective cohort groups defined now by exposure or a source population ___
Retrospective cohort source population and earlier exposure in existing records ___
Case-control ___ reconstruct earlier exposure
Cross-sectional a defined population in one survey window ___

A complete table says:

  • prospective cohort: follow forward for new outcomes;
  • retrospective cohort: read the records forward from earlier exposure to later outcome;
  • case-control: select cases with the outcome and controls without it;
  • cross-sectional: measure current exposure and outcome together.

If you classified every study with old records as case-control, redraw the selection line. Old exposure and later outcome records can form a retrospective cohort.

Mixed comparisons with less support

Name the best-fit design before reading each answer. State the decisive feature, not only the label.

A. A team recruits people who currently use either stairs or lifts by their own choice, records that exposure, and follows new activity outcomes for one year.

This is a prospective cohort: exposure is observed rather than assigned, and the team follows from current exposure to later outcomes. The result would show an association unless additional causal assumptions were justified.

B. Investigators identify every confirmed case of an uncommon equipment-related skin reaction in a registry, select comparable registry members without the reaction, and compare prior glove-material exposure.

This is a case-control study: selection begins with outcome status, then moves backward to earlier exposure. The uncommon outcome makes outcome-first sampling practical. This fictional example does not advise diagnosis or equipment use.

C. A representative campus survey measures current residence noise and current morning-alertness scores during the same week to estimate how common low alertness is and whether it varies with noise.

This is cross-sectional: exposure and outcome are measured together in one window. It can estimate prevalence and association, but the snapshot alone does not establish which came first.

D. A transit agency—not the research team—introduces quieter buses first on routes selected by a maintenance rule written years earlier. Researchers compare outcomes before and after rollout across eligible routes.

This is a natural-experiment candidate, not an automatic causal result. Researchers must test whether the maintenance rule, route eligibility, and other changes make the exposure comparison defensible.

E. A lab team assigns cell cultures to a defined nutrient concentration or comparison condition and records growth under held-alike settings.

This is an experiment because the researchers assign the exposure. Whether assignment is random, whether the comparison condition was run as intended, and whether other settings were held alike affect the strength of the inference.

Misconception
id misconception-retrospective-means-case-control

Commit first. A complete employee roster is grouped by a chemical exposure recorded in 2010, and later diagnoses through 2020 are read from the same archive. Is it case-control because every record is historical?

Tempting shortcut: retrospective data always mean case-control.

Why the shortcut fails: retrospective describes the study's timing relative to recorded events. Case-control describes outcome-first selection. This archive begins with a roster and exposure, then proceeds to later outcomes.

Repair: label the selection point before the calendar. The design is a retrospective cohort.

Recheck: investigators instead select 2020 cases with the diagnosis and controls without it, then compare 2010 exposure. Now the outcome-first entry makes it case-control.

Misconception
id misconception-any-external-event-natural-experiment

Commit first. A voluntary wellness program is offered, and people who already exercise more are more likely to enroll. Is the rollout automatically a natural experiment because researchers did not create it?

Tempting shortcut: anything that happens outside a laboratory is a natural experiment.

Why the shortcut fails: voluntary enrollment may track characteristics that also affect the outcome. An outside event is not enough; the assignment process must create a comparison whose rival differences can be examined and defended.

Repair: describe exactly who became exposed and why. If self-selection remains tied to the outcome, call it an observational cohort or cross-sectional comparison as its timing warrants and limit the conclusion to association.

Recheck: a program begins above a pre-existing eligibility cutoff that participants could not manipulate. This may create a natural-experiment opportunity, but researchers still need to examine the cutoff, other differences, implementation, and outcomes near the boundary.

Independent choice

Write a two-sentence design justification for one new scenario: the first sentence names who creates exposure and how units enter; the second names what inference the design can support and one reason it could be weaker.

If your answer contains only a label, add the assignment and selection verbs. If it claims causation from an ordinary observational association, replace caused with was associated with and name the untested assignment problem. If it says a randomized result is guaranteed, name one implementation or measurement condition that still matters.

Source Note
claims claim-design-assignment, claim-cohort-direction, claim-case-control-use, claim-cross-sectional-use, claim-randomized-inference, claim-natural-assignment, claim-causal-boundarysources source-study-design-overview, source-clinical-design-essentials, source-observational-designs, source-natural-experiments

The sources support the defining assignment and selection features, the uncommon-outcome use of case-control sampling, prevalence-focused cross-sectional use, randomized comparability in expectation, and the need to defend outside assignment in a natural experiment. Every practice scenario and recheck is independently created and fictional.

scenes.title: Test an outside assignment rule
scenes.contentHtml:
Test an outside assignment rule

A fictional regional program offers classroom air-filtration grants to municipalities with an enrollment count just above a cutoff fixed two years earlier. The research team did not create the cutoff or choose recipients. It can obtain authorized, aggregated records of classroom particle concentration before and after installation for municipalities close to either side of the cutoff.

Do not treat the words program or cutoff as the answer. Build the design from the assignment process.

Before reading on, write:

  1. the exposure and outcome;
  2. who assigned exposure;
  3. why municipalities just above and below the cutoff might form a useful comparison;
  4. two facts that would weaken that comparison;
  5. the design label you would use now; and
  6. the strongest conclusion you would permit if the checks were satisfactory.

A defensible analysis

The exposure is receiving the filtration grant; the outcome is the defined change in classroom particle concentration. The program, not the research team, assigned exposure according to an earlier cutoff. Municipalities near either side may be similar enough for the cutoff to create a natural-experiment candidate.

That candidate becomes less persuasive if municipalities could alter their reported enrollment to cross the cutoff, if another program used the same cutoff, if records were collected differently across groups, or if eligibility exceptions tracked expected particle changes. The research team must examine the actual assignment and implementation rather than assuming “above” and “below” are interchangeable.

If the cutoff was fixed, not manipulable, applied consistently, and the nearby groups were otherwise comparable on relevant pre-exposure features, a difference in outcome change could support a bounded causal interpretation of grant assignment near that cutoff. That conclusion would still depend on the stated assumptions and would not automatically generalize to municipalities far from the boundary.

If the assignment process is not defensible, keep the useful part of the question and change the claim. The same records could support an observational cohort comparison of grant exposure followed by later particle outcomes, with association—not causation—as the default interpretation.

This is genuine design choice: the data file may stay the same while the warranted inference changes when the assignment facts change.

Rebuild the full scientific handoff

Complete the final link from the same course navigation aid. Treat the arrows as memory links between course decisions, not as a rule for ordering every investigation:

observation → bounded empirical question → hypothesis → conditions → prediction → variable-and-control audit → ___

The missing link is choose a study design that makes the needed comparison possible.

  • In this map, the question slot names what needs answering.
  • The hypothesis, conditions, and prediction slots record a proposal and the observations expected under its stated conditions.
  • The variable-and-control audit slot makes the intended comparison and rival factors visible.
  • The study-design slot asks who creates the comparison, where it enters time, and how strongly it can support an inference.

The next outcomes examine sampling, randomization, and blinding; measurement quality; correlation and causation; and specific biases. This lesson does not replace those checks. It gives you the first choice: experiment, cohort, case-control, cross-sectional, or natural experiment.

Mastery check

The assessment has four untimed, retakeable items worth twelve points. Attempt each before opening its answer and reasoning. Mastery means earning all twelve points: three for classifying assignment and timing, three for distinguishing retrospective cohort from case-control selection, two for calibrating inference, and four for the external-cutoff transfer. If you miss a criterion, use its feedback to redraw the assignment, selection, and time lines, then retry with the alternate case.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every task can be completed in prose, bullets, or a table. The arrows are also written as sentences, and every table relationship is explained in nearby prose. You may type, dictate into your own device, or use assistive technology; no audio, image upload, dragging, color distinction, speed, laboratory access, or personal data is required. All people, organizations, policies, records, and results are fictional.

Recovery route

If all five labels blur together, ignore the names and answer two ordinary questions: Who created the exposure difference? Did researchers start with exposure, start with outcome, or take one snapshot? Then attach the label.

If prospective and retrospective cohorts split apart, draw exposure before outcome for both. Add “researchers collect forward” to the prospective version and “researchers assemble existing records” to the retrospective version.

If cohort and case-control reverse, name the entry group. Exposure or a source population first means cohort; cases and controls first means case-control.

If cross-sectional seems causal, ask whether the snapshot shows which variable came first. If not, keep the conclusion descriptive or associative.

If every policy looks like a natural experiment, write the assignment rule and two rival reasons for exposure. Without a defensible outside process, use the ordinary observational label and narrow the inference.

If random assignment feels like a guarantee, check whether assignment was implemented, outcomes were measured comparably, and follow-up preserved the intended comparison. Later lessons make those checks more precise.

Source Note
claims claim-design-assignment, claim-cohort-direction, claim-case-control-use, claim-cross-sectional-use, claim-randomized-inference, claim-natural-assignment, claim-causal-boundarysources source-study-design-overview, source-clinical-design-essentials, source-observational-designs, source-natural-experiments

The sources support the design map, randomized and observational inference limits, and the central role of an outside assignment process in natural experiments. The grant cutoff, particle outcome, assignment checks, fallback comparison, and synthesis are original fictional teaching material.

items.prompt: Choose the only complete design map. Case A: researchers assign eligible samples by chance to two temperatures and record later response. Case B: a one-week population survey measures current exposure and current outcome together. Case C: an outside policy assigns exposure using a pre-existing cutoff, and researchers must test whether that rule creates a defensible comparison.

options.text: A randomized experiment; B cross-sectional; C natural-experiment candidate
A prospective cohort; B case-control; C natural experiment with no further assignment checks needed
A randomized experiment; B retrospective cohort; C cross-sectional
A natural experiment; B case-control; C randomized experiment

answer.summary: Researcher random assignment makes A an experiment, simultaneous measurement makes B cross-sectional, and outside cutoff assignment makes C a natural-experiment candidate that still needs checking.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for each correctly classified case in the selected complete map. In A, researchers create exposure groups by random assignment. In B, exposure and outcome are measured in one window. In C, the policy rather than the researchers assigns exposure, but the cutoff's operation and comparability must be defended before stronger inference. All three are correct only in the first option. On a retry, classify: a followed self-selected exposure group, cases selected by an uncommon outcome, and a one-day prevalence survey.
acceptableResponses: The option mapping A to randomized experiment, B to cross-sectional study, and C to a natural-experiment candidate.
commonErrors: Calling researcher random assignment a cohort because outcomes occur later
Calling simultaneous exposure and outcome measurement case-control
Treating every outside cutoff as automatically experiment-like without checking assignment

items.prompt: A complete 2012 factory roster is divided by a noise exposure recorded that year, and existing records are then read forward to hearing-test outcomes recorded through 2022. Name and justify the design. Then state how selection would change if investigators instead began with people who had the 2022 outcome and comparison people who did not, and explain why the word 'historical' does not decide between the two designs.

answer.correct: The roster design is a retrospective cohort because it begins with a defined source population and earlier exposure, then compares subsequent outcomes using records that already exist. If investigators begin by selecting people with and without the 2022 outcome and reconstruct prior noise exposure comparably, it is case-control. Historical data can serve either design; selection and exposure-to-outcome direction decide the label.
answer.summary: Retrospective describes when records are assembled; cohort and case-control describe where selection enters the exposure–outcome sequence.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for naming retrospective cohort and citing source-population or exposure-first entry, one for converting the alternative to outcome-first case-control selection with comparison people from the population that produced the cases, and one for explicitly separating historical timing from selection logic. All three are required for 3/3. If your answer labels both designs retrospective without distinguishing them, draw two arrows: exposure to later outcome for the cohort and outcome to earlier exposure for case-control. On a retry, use old medication records and a later equipment-failure outcome in a complete maintenance fleet.
acceptableResponses: Any equivalent explanation that classifies the roster as retrospective cohort, the outcome-selected alternative as case-control, and makes selection rather than record age decisive.
commonErrors: Calling every use of past records case-control
Failing to identify outcome-first selection in the case-control alternative
Using follow-up as a calendar word without tracing exposure before outcome

items.prompt: Which option gives calibrated conclusions for all three comparisons? (1) A well-implemented randomized experiment with comparable outcome measurement. (2) An ordinary cohort in which participants chose exposure. (3) An outside eligibility rule that may create a natural experiment but whose assignment process has not yet been checked.

options.text: 1 can support a bounded causal inference under its conditions; 2 supports association by default; 3 is not ready for an experiment-like causal claim until assignment and rivals are checked.
All three establish causation because each compares exposed and unexposed groups.
None can inform causal reasoning, including the randomized experiment.
Only 3 establishes causation because outside assignment is always equivalent to random assignment.

answer.summary: Randomized assignment can support stronger causal inference when implementation and measurement hold; self-selected exposure supports association by default; unchecked outside assignment remains only a natural-experiment candidate.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for preserving the randomized experiment's bounded causal strength and one point for correctly limiting both observational cases: association for the self-selected cohort and no experiment-like claim before the outside rule is defended. Both are present only in the calibrated option. On a retry, write one sentence beginning 'because researchers assigned' and one beginning 'because exposure was selected or externally assigned.'
acceptableResponses: The calibrated option distinguishing conditional experimental causality, ordinary observational association, and an unchecked natural-experiment candidate.
commonErrors: Assuming any group comparison establishes causation
Treating random assignment as a guarantee independent of implementation and measurement
Treating an outside event as automatically random or as-if random

items.prompt: A fictional transit authority replaces buses first on routes whose depot score falls just below a cutoff fixed three years earlier. Researchers did not create the rule. They have comparable noise measurements before and after replacement for routes near the cutoff. Identify the best design candidate, name two assignment or implementation facts needed before treating the comparison as experiment-like, state a warranted conclusion if those checks hold, and give the ordinary observational fallback if the cutoff was manipulable or tied to expected noise change.

answer.correct: A complete response identifies a natural-experiment candidate because an outside cutoff assigns replacement. It checks at least two facts such as whether scores could be manipulated, whether the cutoff was applied consistently, whether other programs or exceptions used the same rule, whether nearby routes were comparable before replacement, and whether noise measurement was comparable. If the checks hold, a difference in noise change can support a bounded causal interpretation of replacement assignment for routes near the cutoff. If assignment is not defensible, the records support an observational retrospective cohort comparison of earlier replacement exposure followed by later noise outcome, interpreted as association.
answer.summary: The design and conclusion depend on the outside assignment rule, not merely on the existence of a cutoff or before-and-after data.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for identifying and justifying the natural-experiment candidate, one for two concrete assignment or implementation checks, one for a bounded conditional causal interpretation near the cutoff, and one for the retrospective-cohort fallback with an association-only default. All four are required for 4/4. If your answer says only 'natural experiment,' inspect who could cross the cutoff and what else changed. On a retry, evaluate warehouse lighting upgrades staggered by a maintenance calendar fixed before later electricity use was recorded.
acceptableResponses: Any equivalent response that ties the natural-experiment candidate and conditional inference to a defensible external cutoff and supplies an observational cohort fallback when that assignment fails.
commonErrors: Calling the design a randomized experiment even though researchers did not assign routes
Naming a natural experiment without checking the assignment process
Generalizing a near-cutoff comparison to every route
Keeping causal language after changing to an ordinary observational cohort

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when researcher random assignment in Case A is classified as a randomized experiment.
Award 1 point only when simultaneous one-window measurement in Case B is classified as cross-sectional.
Award 1 point only when Case C is classified as a natural-experiment candidate whose outside assignment still requires checking.
Award 1 point only when the roster is classified as retrospective cohort with population- or exposure-first entry followed by later outcome.
Award 1 point only when the alternative is classified as case-control because selection begins with cases and comparison people without the outcome.
Award 1 point only when historical record timing is explicitly separated from the selection logic that distinguishes the designs.
Award 1 point only when the randomized experiment is allowed a bounded causal interpretation under implementation and measurement conditions rather than treated as a guarantee.
Award 1 point only when the self-selected cohort is limited to association by default and the unchecked outside rule is kept as a candidate rather than automatic causal evidence.
Award 1 point only when the outside cutoff justifies a natural-experiment candidate rather than a researcher-randomized experiment.
Award 1 point only when at least two concrete checks address manipulation, consistent application, co-occurring changes, pre-exposure comparability, or comparable measurement.
Award 1 point only when any causal interpretation is conditional, bounded to the assignment and routes near the cutoff, and linked to the stated checks.
Award 1 point only when failed assignment checks lead to a retrospective observational cohort description and an association-only default conclusion.

claims.statement: An experiment assigns an intervention or exposure under researcher control, whereas an observational study records exposure differences not assigned by the researcher; assignment and outcome timing help distinguish the study design.
claims.scope: Introductory classification of analytic study designs by researcher assignment, selection point, and outcome timing.
claims.uncertainty: Hybrid and specialized designs may need more detailed classification than this introductory decision sequence provides.

claims.statement: A cohort design starts with a defined population or exposure status and compares subsequent outcomes; prospective cohorts collect forward, while retrospective cohorts reconstruct the same exposure-to-later-outcome order from existing records.
claims.scope: Standard prospective and retrospective cohort logic at college-intro level.
claims.uncertainty: Nested and case-cohort variants are outside this lesson.

claims.statement: A case-control design selects cases and comparison controls from the population that produced the cases by outcome status, reconstructs prior exposure comparably, and can make outcome-first sampling practical for uncommon outcomes.
claims.scope: Introductory, non-nested case-control studies with a defined outcome and a comparison group without that outcome.
claims.uncertainty: Control selection and exposure ascertainment determine validity but are reserved for later appraisal outcomes.

claims.statement: A cross-sectional design measures exposure and outcome in a defined population at one time or brief window, can estimate prevalence, and usually cannot establish which variable came first.
claims.scope: Typical one-time descriptive or analytic cross-sectional studies.
claims.uncertainty: Some cross-sectional instruments collect limited historical timing, but the basic snapshot alone lacks temporal order.

claims.statement: Random assignment makes intervention groups comparable in expectation and can strengthen causal inference, but chance imbalance, implementation, adherence, measurement, and other assumptions still limit the conclusion.
claims.scope: Randomized comparisons of a defined intervention with an appropriate comparison condition.
claims.uncertainty: The lesson does not teach allocation concealment, adherence estimands, missingness, or measurement quality.

claims.statement: A natural experiment uses exposure variation created outside researcher control; its causal persuasiveness depends on understanding and defending the assignment process rather than merely calling an event natural.
claims.scope: Natural-experiment opportunities arising from external policies, rules, events, or implementation processes.
claims.uncertainty: Published definitions vary from broad external exposure changes to narrow as-if-random assignment; the lesson uses a strict decision boundary for causal language.

claims.statement: An association observed without researcher-controlled or otherwise defensible assignment does not by itself establish that the exposure caused the outcome.
claims.scope: Introductory interpretation of ordinary observational comparisons and natural experiments.
claims.uncertainty: Formal causal inference from observational data can be possible under additional designs and assumptions that are beyond this lesson.

terms.definition: The intervention, condition, characteristic, or experience whose relationship with an outcome is being compared.
The response, event, state, or measurement that a study records and compares.
A study in which researchers assign an intervention or exposure and compare outcomes under the assigned conditions.
Allocation by a chance process that gives each eligible unit a known chance of receiving each study condition and makes groups comparable in expectation.
A study in which researchers do not assign the exposure being compared but record exposure and outcome differences that occur.
An observational study that begins with a defined population or exposure groups and compares subsequent outcomes.
A cohort assembled before the outcomes of interest occur and followed forward as new outcomes are recorded.
A cohort assembled from existing records that preserve an earlier exposure followed by a later outcome.
An observational study that selects cases with an outcome and comparison controls without it from the population that produced the cases, then compares earlier exposure.
A study that measures exposure and outcome in a defined population at one time or over one brief window.

A study of exposure variation created by an external event, policy, rule, or implementation process rather than assigned by the research team; the assignment process must be examined before making experiment-like claims.
The proportion of a defined population with a specified condition or characteristic at a stated time or over a stated period.
A reasoned conclusion about whether changing an exposure would change an outcome, under stated assumptions and conditions.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson identity

  • Lesson: PREM-SCI-004 — Choosing a study design: assignment, selection, and time
  • Accountable principal: VSBDev (github:VSBDev)
  • Course-content licence: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)

Material agent assistance

VSBDev is accountable for the contribution. Material authoring assistance used OpenAI Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, version Codex agent runtime 2026-07-20, in isolated author run 75e91d84-f365-4bd6-93b8-98193d793259.

The material-instructions digest is sha256:3fae846760b89862f1d82ad1d78aab6006bf4007cba6dc1461c804be96a13a2a. It covers the exact UTF-8 task payload supplied by the root agent, including its final newline, and excludes hidden provider and system instructions.

One-pass material finalization used OpenAI Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, version Codex agent runtime 2026-07-20, in fresh isolated run 83ae14a9-bdcd-4619-a755-9e83ff8a2fe4. Its material-instructions digest is sha256:8cefd99223994f6813e3df7df5d659bb942f459e7809b855278a02831dc153b1 under the same exact-payload boundary.

Current 0.1.1 one-pass finalization is recorded in adjudication.json under fresh run codex-finalizer-sci004-A7BD0CB1-D250-45EC-8BD3-788D6C950E87 with material-instructions digest sha256:10e511061f809fbd2079d481459e2811cc9572efcc9342bfe28222fe7adb95bd. The operator supplies its runtime-verified system, provider, model, and version in that artifact from the runtime banner.

Original synthesis and source use

The lesson's prose, residence-hall opening, employer archive, transit and grant cases, materials and registry cases, tables, prompts, assessment items, feedback, and instructional sequence are original course material created for this pack. The sources in references.json were used only to verify factual study-design distinctions and inference limits. No source wording, organization, example, question, table, figure, media, or dataset was copied, translated, adapted, or redistributed.

Third-party assets

No third-party image, diagram, audio, video, dataset, or other asset is included. The pack contains only original text and structured records.

Source-access attestation

Before substantive access on 2026-07-20, the accountable author checked the public PMC robots route and NCBI website and data usage policies. The accessed article and API routes were allowed, no agent-specific restriction was found, and no login, paywall, rate limit, or technical access control was bypassed. Source licences are recorded for transparency; every use in this lesson remains facts-only original expression.

Modifications

This 0.1.1 finalization preserves the reviewed study-design model and makes one bounded revision after academic and learning-design advisory reviews of candidate 98d9269eebf04c20e0efe08e4fa91b422316ac85: it frames the cumulative inquiry chain as a course navigation aid rather than a universal scientific sequence, completes the cross-sectional claim mapping in the worked example, gives the practice table a timing-neutral header, and restores candidate-stage claim review statuses pending the maintainer's publication transition. The final decision and complete finding dispositions are recorded separately in adjudication.json.
